stimuli.description: Letter image matching the letter presented N trials ago (case-insensitive).
Letter image NOT matching the letter presented N trials ago, OR an early-block filler trial (first delay trials, condition='N/A') which is also responded to as mismatch.

phase_detection.feedback: (function(){var c=document.querySelector('#jspsych-content');if(!c)return false;var t=c.textContent||'';if(document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus img.center'))return false;return /Press\s*enter\s*to\s*(begin|continue)/i.test(t)||/Done with this task/i.test(t)||/Please take this time to read your feedback/i.test(t)||/We will now start/i.test(t)||/We are now going to repeat/i.test(t)||/Thanks for completing this task/i.test(t);})()

task_specific.notes: window.correctResponse holds the correct key for the current trial (auto-handles counterbalancing via group_index 0-4 vs 5-14). The first 'delay' trials of each block are forced to require the mismatch response (condition='N/A' in source) — handled here by routing 'N/A' to the mismatch stimulus rule.

citations.authors: Kane, M. J., Conway, A. R. A., Miura, T. K., & Colflesh, G. J. H.
citations.title: Working memory, attention control, and the N-back task: A question of construct validity
citations.quote: Mean correct RTs to targets in the 2-back ranged in the high-500-ms to mid-600-ms range across samples.

citations.authors: Jaeggi, S. M., Buschkuehl, M., Perrig, W. J., & Meier, B.
citations.title: The concurrent validity of the N-back task as a working memory measure
citations.quote: Target-trial RTs in 2-back averaged ~600 ms in healthy young adults.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: Mu estimates for choice-RT working-memory tasks clustered between roughly 480 and 560 ms.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: Sigma estimates for working-memory choice-RT tasks clustered around 50–80 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.table_or_figure: Figure 4 (ex-Gaussian sigma distribution across simulated diffusion sets)
citations.quote: Sigma values from diffusion-generated ex-Gaussian fits typically fell between 40 and 90 ms for choice-RT.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: Tau estimates for WM choice-RT tasks were larger than non-WM analogs, falling roughly between 80 and 160 ms.

citations.authors: Hockey, A., & Geffen, G.
citations.title: The concurrent validity and test-retest reliability of a visuospatial working memory task
citations.quote: Right-tail (slow-response) contributions in n-back trial-level RTs were notably elevated relative to standard choice-RT.

match.rationale: N-back match (target) RTs in healthy adults typically center around 580–650 ms when collapsed across 1- and 2-back delays. Ex-Gaussian fits to working-memory choice-RT data consistently yield mu in the ~500–550 ms range, sigma ~60–80 ms, and a moderate tau (~100–140 ms) capturing the right tail driven by occasional WM-comparison delays. Match trials carry a slight cost over mismatch because they require confirming a positive identity comparison.

citations.authors: Kane, M. J., Conway, A. R. A., Miura, T. K., & Colflesh, G. J. H.
citations.title: Working memory, attention control, and the N-back task: A question of construct validity
citations.quote: Non-target (mismatch) RTs were systematically faster than target RTs by approximately 20–40 ms.

citations.authors: Jaeggi, S. M., Buschkuehl, M., Perrig, W. J., & Meier, B.
citations.title: The concurrent validity of the N-back task as a working memory measure
citations.quote: Mean RTs for non-target trials in 2-back centered around 540–580 ms.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: For frequent-response categories sigma was modestly smaller than for rare/target categories.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Sigma estimates remained within the 40–85 ms band across response categories.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: Tau on majority/non-target categories was lower than on target/identity-confirmation categories.

citations.authors: Hockey, A., & Geffen, G.
citations.title: The concurrent validity and test-retest reliability of a visuospatial working memory task
citations.quote: Tail mass for non-target responses fell in the 70–150 ms range across participants.

mismatch.rationale: Mismatch (non-target) responses are the default category and are typically 20–40 ms faster than matches in n-back. Mu ~480–500 ms is consistent with published ex-Gaussian fits; sigma slightly smaller than match reflecting somewhat tighter Gaussian core; tau a bit lower because the ex-Gaussian tail is most pronounced on identity-confirmation trials. Includes the early-block 'N/A' filler trials, which are easy and pull the central tendency slightly faster.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/fα noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Lag-1 autocorrelations of single-trial RT in choice tasks typically fell between approximately 0.05 and 0.20.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Short-range serial dependence in RT was small in absolute magnitude but consistently positive across tasks.

autocorrelation.rationale: Trial-to-trial RT autocorrelation (phi ~0.05–0.15) is well documented across speeded-choice tasks including n-back. A modest AR(1) coefficient of 0.10 captures short-range serial dependence in attention/arousal without overstating the effect.

condition_repetition.rationale: Pure match/mismatch repetition effects are not a canonical, robust feature of n-back. Sequential effects in n-back are dominated by lure/target structure (e.g., n+1 / n-1 lures) rather than a clean repetition benefit, and enabling a generic repetition facilitation/cost would mis-specify the effect. Disabled to avoid spurious bias.

citations.authors: Lim, J., & Dinges, D. F.
citations.title: Sleep deprivation and vigilant attention
citations.quote: Within-session RT slopes during 10–20 minute sustained-attention tasks correspond to roughly 0.1–0.6 ms per trial in rested adults.

citations.authors: Lim, J., & Dinges, D. F.
citations.title: A meta-analysis of the impact of short-term sleep deprivation on cognitive variables
citations.quote: Time-on-task RT increases were modest in well-rested participants over short sessions.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Across 120 test trials plus practice (~10–15 min), healthy adults show a mild positive RT drift attributable to time-on-task fatigue and vigilance decrement. ~0.3 ms/trial yields ~36 ms cumulative slowing across the test block, consistent with reported within-session drift in working-memory paradigms.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: The slow (low-frequency) component of RT variability accounted for amplitude on the order of 10–20 ms in choice tasks.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/fα noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Estimated 1/f component magnitudes corresponded to standard deviations on the order of a few percent of mean RT.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Spectral slopes corresponded to Hurst exponents in the 0.7–0.9 range across cognitive tasks.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/fα noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Hurst estimates in choice-RT data clustered around 0.75–0.90 with substantial individual variation.

pink_noise.rationale: 1/f^alpha (pink) noise in RT time series is a near-universal finding (Gilden, Wagenmakers); n-back time series exhibit Hurst exponents in the 0.8–0.9 range. SD of ~15 ms captures slow attention fluctuations layered on top of trial-level Gaussian noise.

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M. A.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: RTs on the trial immediately following an error were systematically slower than baseline by tens of milliseconds.

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., van Ravenzwaaij, D., Nieuwenhuis, S., van der Maas, H. L. J., Forstmann, B. U., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowing: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Robust PES estimates ranged from approximately 20 ms to 80 ms across choice-RT datasets.

citations.authors: Notebaert, W., Houtman, F., Van Opstal, F., Gevers, W., Fias, W., & Verguts, T.
citations.title: Post-error slowing: An orienting account
citations.quote: Per-subject PES magnitudes occasionally exceeded 100 ms in tasks with infrequent errors.

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., van Ravenzwaaij, D., Nieuwenhuis, S., van der Maas, H. L. J., Forstmann, B. U., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowing: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Upper-bound PES values approached 80–100 ms across analyzed datasets.

post_error_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing of ~30–80 ms is robustly observed in n-back and related WM/choice-RT tasks (Rabbitt; Dutilh et al.). The range reflects between-trial and between-subject variability while staying within typical healthy-adult magnitudes.

post_interrupt_slowing.rationale: N-back has no within-trial interrupt structure. Inter-block attention checks are infrequent and well-separated; treating them as 'interrupts' for trial-level slowing is not supported by the n-back literature. Disabled.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SD in mean RT for healthy-adult n-back is typically 80–110 ms; 90 ms is a central estimate. Per-condition residual SD ~30 ms captures individual differences in match-vs-mismatch cost. Sigma/tau multiplicative jitter of ±25% spans the bulk of the population without producing extreme tail subjects. Accuracy SD of 0.05 matches reported between-subject variability for 1/2-back in healthy adults; clip floor at 0.55 is just above chance (50% binary choice) to exclude non-compliant subjects while allowing genuinely poor performers. Omission SD of 0.015 with a clip ceiling of 0.12 reflects that healthy adults rarely omit more than ~10% within a 1500 ms response window in n-back.

citations.authors: Hultsch, D. F., MacDonald, S. W. S., & Dixon, R. A.
citations.title: Variability in reaction time performance of younger and older adults
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of mean RT in healthy young adults on choice-RT tasks fell in the 70–110 ms range.

citations.authors: Jaeggi, S. M., Buschkuehl, M., Perrig, W. J., & Meier, B.
citations.title: The concurrent validity of the N-back task as a working memory measure
citations.quote: Inter-individual SD of mean n-back RT was on the order of 80–100 ms.

citations.authors: Kane, M. J., Conway, A. R. A., Miura, T. K., & Colflesh, G. J. H.
citations.title: Working memory, attention control, and the N-back task: A question of construct validity
citations.quote: Match-vs-mismatch RT differences varied across participants by approximately 20–40 ms.

citations.authors: Hultsch, D. F., MacDonald, S. W. S., & Dixon, R. A.
citations.title: Variability in reaction time performance of younger and older adults
citations.quote: Per-condition residual variability around the subject mean was substantially smaller than the overall mean SD.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: Subject-level sigma and tau ranged from approximately 0.7× to 1.4× of group-mean values.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Plausible inter-individual sigma/tau ratios spanned roughly 0.7–1.3 of the central estimate.

citations.authors: Jaeggi, S. M., Buschkuehl, M., Perrig, W. J., & Meier, B.
citations.title: The concurrent validity of the N-back task as a working memory measure
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of accuracy in 1-back and 2-back was on the order of 0.04–0.08 in healthy adults.

citations.authors: Owen, A. M., McMillan, K. M., Laird, A. R., & Bullmore, E.
citations.title: N-back working memory paradigm: A meta-analysis of normative functional neuroimaging studies
citations.quote: Healthy-adult accuracy SDs across reviewed studies were typically below 0.10.

citations.authors: Kane, M. J., Conway, A. R. A., Miura, T. K., & Colflesh, G. J. H.
citations.title: Working memory, attention control, and the N-back task: A question of construct validity
citations.quote: Omission rates within constrained response windows showed between-subject SD of roughly 1–3 percentage points.

citations.authors: Hultsch, D. F., MacDonald, S. W. S., & Dixon, R. A.
citations.title: Variability in reaction time performance of younger and older adults
citations.quote: Miss/omission variability across young adults was small but non-negligible at constrained deadlines.

citations.authors: Owen, A. M., McMillan, K. M., Laird, A. R., & Bullmore, E.
citations.title: N-back working memory paradigm: A meta-analysis of normative functional neuroimaging studies
citations.quote: Accuracy on 1-back and 2-back rarely fell below ~0.60 in healthy compliant samples and rarely exceeded ~0.98.

citations.authors: Jaeggi, S. M., Buschkuehl, M., Perrig, W. J., & Meier, B.
citations.title: The concurrent validity of the N-back task as a working memory measure
citations.quote: Healthy young-adult accuracy spanned approximately 0.65 to 0.98 across 1-back and 2-back.

citations.authors: Kane, M. J., Conway, A. R. A., Miura, T. K., & Colflesh, G. J. H.
citations.title: Working memory, attention control, and the N-back task: A question of construct validity
citations.quote: Omission rates in compliant healthy adults rarely exceeded ~10% within ~1500 ms response windows.

citations.authors: Jaeggi, S. M., Buschkuehl, M., Perrig, W. J., & Meier, B.
citations.title: The concurrent validity of the N-back task as a working memory measure
citations.quote: Trial-level omissions in healthy participants were typically near zero and seldom above ~0.10.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'N-back (RDoC)' with 2 stimuli. Source files: jquery.min.js, bootstrap.min.js, math.min.js, jspsych.js, plugin-instructions.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-html-button-response.js, plugin-survey-text.js, plugin-fullscreen.js, plugin-.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 4 temporal effects.
Produced 39 citations across 19 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 25 parameters; 6 are high-sensitivity.
